does: This paper introduces a consensus-labeled prompt bank that systematically distinguishes between requests for malicious code versus harmful security knowledge, creating a standardized evaluation framework for measuring how well coding models refuse dangerous requests. The authors validate that their CODE-versus-KNOWLEDGE classification remains stable across a substantially expanded corpus pool and independently refreshed judge panels, demonstrating this distinction captures a genuine phenomenon rather than corpus or annotator artifacts. By consolidating eight diverse corpora spanning multiple elicitation paradigms including direct requests, jailbreak-decorated prompts, indirect requests, and agent/interpreter approaches, the work establishes reproducible metrics for assessing refusal rates across comparable conditions.
connects: This work builds on prior research establishing that general-purpose language models face distinct refusal challenges compared to specialized coding models, extending foundational benchmark studies that began fragmenting malicious code evaluation across incomparable datasets and methodologies. The paper responds to the growing recognition that coding models present asymmetric risks—where compliance produces executable weapons rather than merely harmful text—by building on earlier four-corpus releases that identified the CODE-versus-KNOWLEDGE distinction. It combines insights from adversarial robustness literature, jailbreak research, and software security evaluation, while addressing limitations in existing refusal benchmarks that lack systematic classification schemes or cross-dataset consistency.
enables: This standardized prompt bank enables rigorous empirical comparison of how different coding models handle malicious requests, supporting more principled safety evaluation before deployment. The stable CODE-versus-KNOWLEDGE classification framework allows researchers to precisely measure whether coding models maintain appropriately high refusal bars compared to general-purpose systems, facilitating targeted safety improvements for specific request categories. The consolidated benchmark infrastructure with independent judge validation enables future work to identify which elicitation paradigms are most effective at inducing compliance, probe whether specific model architectures or training methods improve refusal behavior, and establish industry standards for responsible deployment of coding-specialized systems.
